title: Does Aspirating Work？
excerpt: The reality is there's almost an infinite variation of the facial artery because little wiggles here and there are not documented as differences.

segments.text: The reality is there's almost an infinite variation of the facial artery because little wiggles here and there are not documented as differences. I guess the people who don't want who don't like aspirating would say that probabilistically maybe it helps but is that probability does it outweigh the risk of doing an additional intervention? So the additional the trauma of aspirating for example yeah I actually haven't seen that come up. Usually it's more of a discussion around but that's a very good way of thinking about it. But that usually it's a discussion more around whether it works or not. Yeah. And and why do they say it doesn't work? So one of the reasons it doesn't work is if your fill is too thick. So we did an experiment on this. This is one of the reasons why I did the experiment, which is um this idea that when you're injecting, if you've primed the needle, it no longer is sensitive to any aspirating. Or if it is, you've got to aspirate for the very minimum 10 seconds, which is actually a very long time. If you're doing a big procedure, your injection takes 1 second, your aspirating takes 10

seconds. So, it lengthens the time of your procedure enormously if you do that for every injection. So, people don't like doing it. But um but all of those are uh they're not probabilistic in the way that they're phrasing it. They're saying it just there's no point unless this um and actually when we tested it with the fillers that we got a range of different results. So many of the fillers very reassuringly aspirate within a second. So very quickly and this was in my own forearm. We'll we'll post a link in the video um so that you can see that experiment or get the free download which where we have the timing of different fillers. it revealed to me is that every filler has a different probability here of aspirating in a different situation. And then if you change the needle, that's another diff different probability. And of course, different areas of the face have different arteries, different anatomy. And I don't just mean according to what the textbook says. I mean there's differences between individuals. We just don't match the textbook. Huge variation. I know there's something like 40 different variations of the facial artery, but those are simplified variations. The reality is there's

almost an infinite variation of the facial artery because little wiggles here and there are not documented as differences. Um but they are so we all have different anatomy and that means you've got to think about you got to think approach the whole thing as in is there a way I can make a tiny incremental difference in the risk of this procedure which makes sense to me and apply that as one of many different steps. So it's not this idea that aspirating makes you safe and not aspirating makes you unsafe. It's that it's one of many different barriers you put in the way of a potential uh vascular occlusion. Not a not a single one step saved you from all the problems cuz obviously that's just not how it works.
